ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for 'ebiko mayonnaise grilled sea bass set meal'; mapped to closest parent class 'fish-based dish' (FOODON_00002809). A more specific term for Japanese grilled fish set meals does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fish products — grilled fish (composite dish)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ used as closest defensible anchor for a grilled fish composite set meal. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for a Japanese teishoku-style grilled sea bass set with ebiko mayonnaise and rice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept for this composite Japanese set meal dish.

serving.description: 1 set meal (grilled sea bass fillet with ebiko mayo, steamed rice, and sides)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal portion of approximately 650 g, comprising a grilled sea bass fillet topped with ebiko mayonnaise, a bowl of steamed white rice, and accompanying sides such as salad or miso soup.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175159) and White Rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756), combined with mayonnaise and shrimp roe component estimates
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Japanese set meal. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted combination of USDA FoodData Central entries: grilled sea bass (FDC ID 175159, ~35% of dish weight), cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756, ~35% of dish weight), mayonnaise (FDC ID 172741, ~5% of dish weight), and shrimp/fish roe (FDC ID 175180, ~5% of dish weight), with remaining weight (~20%) attributed to salad vegetables and miso soup. Input per_100g values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA-derived composite estimate and found broadly consistent; USDA-derived values are used as the authoritative basis. Sodium reflects soy sauce and seasoning contribution.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods: a Physiological Basis for Carbohydrate Exchange — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008); University of Sydney GI Database — White rice, boiled (GI ~64)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. GI estimated at 64 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (GI ~64, medium, per University of Sydney GI Database). The grilled sea bass, mayonnaise, and vegetable components contribute negligible carbohydrates and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (64) × available carbohydrate per serving (60 g from 650 g × 9.2 g/100g) ÷ 100 = 38.4 g. This is a high GL, consistent with a rice-dominant set meal.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium from soy sauce, ebiko mayonnaise, and seasoning estimated at 185 mg/100g (approximately 1,203 mg per 650 g serving), approaching the 1,500 mg daily DASH target in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce, limit the ebiko mayonnaise portion, and avoid adding extra condiments, as the full set meal provides approximately 80% of the recommended daily sodium limit for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Steamed white rice is the dominant carbohydrate source with a GI of approximately 64 (medium) and a high glycemic load of ~38 per serving; dietary fibre is low at 0.5 g/100g, limiting postprandial glucose attenuation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown or mixed-grain rice to lower the glycemic index and increase fibre intake, and should limit or omit any tempura component to reduce refined carbohydrate load and postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.2 g/100g; sea bass provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (~2.8 g/100g polyunsaturated fat); however, sodium is elevated and mayonnaise contributes omega-6 fatty acids.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume this dish in moderation, prioritising the grilled sea bass for its omega-3 content, while requesting reduced soy sauce and a smaller ebiko mayonnaise portion to manage sodium and omega-6 intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: If tempura is included, deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation per EULAR dietary guidelines; sea bass provides modest omega-3 fatty acids.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request the set meal without tempura and with reduced ebiko mayonnaise to minimise AGE exposure and omega-6 fatty acid intake, while benefiting from the anti-inflammatory omega-3 content of the grilled sea bass.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Ebiko Mayonnaise Grilled Sea Bass Set Meal (えびこマヨネーズ焼き鱸定食) is a Japanese-style teishoku (定食) set meal served at Japanese restaurants in Singapore. The centrepiece is a fillet of sea bass (suzuki, 鱸) that has been grilled until the skin is lightly charred and the flesh is moist and flaky, then topped with a savoury-sweet ebiko (shrimp roe) mayonnaise sauce that adds a briny, umami-rich flavour and a slightly creamy texture. The set is typically accompanied by a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, and may include sides such as a green salad, miso soup, or pickled vegetables. The overall flavour profile is mild, savoury, and umami-forward, with the richness of the mayonnaise balanced by the clean, delicate taste of the grilled fish. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 131 kcal per 100 g, with 8.5 g protein from the sea bass, 6.9 g fat (predominantly from mayonnaise and fish oils), and 9.2 g carbohydrate primarily from the rice component. The sea bass contributes beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids. Sodium is moderate at 185 mg/100g but accumulates to approximately 1,200 mg per full serving due to soy sauce and seasoning. The glycemic index is estimated at 64 (medium) driven by the white rice component, yielding a high glycemic load of approximately 38 per serving. Clinically, this dish is suitable for most healthy adults in moderate portions; individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease should request brown rice, reduced soy sauce, and a smaller mayonnaise portion to optimise the nutritional profile.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Japanese set meal. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted combination of USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled sea bass, cooked white rice, mayonnaise, and shrimp roe, cross-checked against the input ai_generated values (confidence 0.7). The composite estimate is internally consistent and plausible, but cannot be verified against a direct approved-source measurement for this specific dish. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted USDA FoodData Central composite (grilled sea bass FDC 175159, cooked white rice FDC 169756, mayonnaise FDC 172741, shrimp roe FDC 175180) as no single approved-source entry exists for this dish. Input ai_generated values were cross-checked and found broadly consistent. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available from the composite USDA source for this preparation): vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Detailed micronutrient breakdown for this specific composite Japanese set meal is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.8) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central grilled sea bass and mayonnaise fatty acid profiles; these are approximate. 4. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate source (steamed white rice, University of Sydney GI Database). 5. The dish may optionally include tempura; if tempura is present, fat, carbohydrate, and energy values will be higher than reported. 6. Halal status is unconfirmed as ebiko (shrimp roe) and mayonnaise sourcing are restaurant-dependent. 7. All core macros (protein_g 8.5, fat_g 6.9, carbohydrate_g 9.2) are non-zero and supported by the USDA composite estimate.
